project_title_en: Client Reporting and Engagement System (CRES/ReportIn) / Système de Rapports et d’engagement des Clients (SREC)/JeMePrésente
branch: Intelligence and Enforcement / Renseignement et exécution de la loi
description_en:
Background

Canada’s immigration detention program is governed by the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA) and the Immigration and Refugee Protection Regulations (IRPR). The Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) is responsible for monitoring and enforcing conditions imposed on individuals in Canada under the IRPA and the IRPR.

The National Immigration Detention Framework (NIDF) was approved by Parliament in 2016 to create a better, fairer immigration detention system that supports the humane and dignified treatment of individuals while protecting public safety. Immigration detention is based on the principle that detention shall be used only as a measure of last resort, in limited circumstances. In certain cases, however, detention may be deemed necessary when: 
1.	an individual presents a risk to public safety (i.e. when an individual has a documented history or pattern of violent or aggressive crimes/behaviour), 
2.	the CBSA is unable to establish an individual’s legitimate identity, and/or 
3.	the behaviour of the individual threatens program integrity (i.e. such as immigration proceedings or removal). 

When detention must take place, the CBSA uses improved risk assessment processes, developed through the NIDF, to ensure that detainees are placed in the most appropriate facility at the time of detention. 

Several factors must be considered when deciding to detain an individual, including the availability and potential use of all alternatives to detention (ATDs). Over the last eight years, the CBSA has reduced the use of detention by increasing ATDs. ATDs, such as in-person reporting, Community Case Management and Supervision, and Electronic Monitoring, are used to effectively release individuals into the communities while ensuring public safety and immigration program integrity.  

The CBSA has now further expanded ATDs by developing a voluntary smartphone app named ReportIn that allows foreign nationals and permanent residents subject to immigration enforcement conditions to report to the CBSA remotely.

The app uses facial comparison and geographic data to verify the identity and location of individuals.  

The app was developed in compliance with Government of Canada standards on applications for mobile devices.  In addition, feedback from the Privacy Commissioner was included in the development of the app and a Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) has been completed.  

About ReportIn/CRES
Reporting conditions are imposed by the CBSA, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) or the Immigration and Refugee Board (IRB) on a case-by-case basis under the IRPA. A common condition imposed is the requirement to report on a regular reporting schedule to the CBSA. The voluntary CBSA ReportIn app will allow individuals subject to reporting conditions to report to the CBSA without physically attending a CBSA office. The ReportIn app will provide a secure process for individuals to report to the CBSA without coming in person to one of our offices while also enhancing the CBSA ability monitor individuals who report remotely.

CBSA CRES/ReportIn solution consists of:
•	A voluntary public-facing smartphone app, ReportIn, where individuals submit reports using a mobile device/smartphone and facial images as a form of authenticating the individuals’ identity. The individuals’ facial biometrics and location, provided by sensors and/or the GPS in the mobile device/smartphone, are recorded when they report through the ReportIn app and is sent to the cloud-based processing and monitoring system, CRES.
•	A Cloud-based processing and monitoring backend system, CRES, allows CBSA immigration enforcement personnel to monitor, contact and assess an individual’s reporting facial images, locations, reporting frequency, and compliance history without individuals having to physically attend a CBSA office.

Using the mobile app, the individual submits photos that are then compared to a reference photo and a similarity score is generated by a facial comparison algorithm. In addition to the automated similarity scoring, CBSA trained staff will review, for every report made by an individual, the submitted photos and a determination will be made whether the submitted photos are a match to the individual. 

The individuals’ location is also collected every time they report and if the individual fails to comply with their conditions. 

ReportIn also allows for one-way CBSA direct messaging with individuals, which will make communication with the individual using the ReportIn app easier.

Other than facial biometric data, the supplemental types of personal information collected by ReportIn includes given and family name, Unique Client Identifier (UCI), personal email address, phone number, employment information (if applicable), facial photos, ad hoc report text, location, device ID, device model, device operating system version, error message, and trace information (where error happened).  

As previously stated, an individuals’ location will be collected and recorded when the following events occur: 
•	When an individual submits their report on their scheduled reporting date, the location, sent by sensors and/or the GPS in the mobile device/smartphone, is recorded when an individual reports through the ReportIn app and is sent to the cloud-based processing and monitoring system CRES. The EnStream location will also be used and connect only to the CRES backend system.  The EnStream service operates externally from and is not connected to the ReportIn app however may be used to collect location when an investigation is opened and the individual has not complied with remote reporting conditions. 

The operational benefits of the project are:
•	Improved management of the inventory of individuals the CBSA must monitor up until they are removed from Canada or the individual becomes a permanent resident of Canada, a Canadian Citizen, granted protected person status in Canada or their temporary resident status is restored. 
•	The ReportIn validation process is much more time efficient than an in-person meeting and provides greater operational flexibility in light of fluctuating volumes of clients.  As such, the ReportIn app is expected to increase monitoring capability while streamlining costs. 

The benefits to the individuals are:
•	Improved communication, transparency and allowing more individuals an alternative to in-person reporting and improving the engagement with the CBSA. 
•	Facilitating a secure process for individuals to enroll and report to the CBSA.
•	Improving support to individuals with accessibility needs where they do not need to attend a CBSA office and providing a more accessible means to report e.g. less time off work, reduced child care cost, reduced transportation cost and/or travel time etc.  

During enrollment the CBSA staff verifies the individual during an in-person or virtual meeting. 

ReportIn uses Amazon Rekognition, a facial recognition software, to perform a comparison of a photo of a known individual against a reference photo. This comparison generates a similarity score, indicating if the individual who is reporting is in fact the individual on reporting conditions.  The software comparison performed is a one-to-one comparison and, to note, is not a one to many comparison. It is also important to note that the software does not compare images against a database of publicly collected images.

All submissions will undergo 100% human oversight. This approach ensures that human judgment and discretion are applied to the results, promoting accountability and accuracy in the system's operation.

There are three levels of review. If a facial comparison match is not confirmed, the Alternatives to Detention Monitoring Centre Officer (ATD MCO), of the Alternatives to Detention Monitoring Centre (ATD MC), may contact the individual within a day or two for a follow up investigation. If the ATD MCO is still unable to confirm a facial comparison match, they will refer the case to the Alternatives to Detention Monitoring Centre Supervisor (ATD MCS). If the ATD MCS has additional questions or they are unable to confirm a facial comparison match, they will refer the case to the Regional Inland Enforcement office for investigation by an Inland Enforcement Officer (IEO). If the follow up investigation results in the IEO determining that the individual has not complied with their conditions, the individual could be detained as per the IRPA and IRPR.

The benefits to the individual and CBSA includes process optimization and workflow automation. The software provides process optimization by analyzing large images (data sets) to identify anomalies which assists in providing workflow automation. Workflow automation allows CBSA to utilize resources in other capacities due to the remote reporting option by not requiring individuals to attend the CBSA office.
program: Border Enforcement / Detentions / Exécution de la loi à la frontière / Détentions

", "businessDrivers3": "The client needs the system will address are:
•	Remote reporting instead of in-person reporting will save the individual time and money by not having to travel or arrange transportation or childcare, as well as save on time away from employment.
•	Allow more individuals with a remote reporting option as the voluntary app does not require a data plan, only access to Wi-Fi. 
•	Improved accuracy of verification of identity and location given the improved technology. 
•	Increased compliance from individuals as remote reporting is easier to access than in-person reporting which will increase the compliance rates.  
•	Provide eligible individuals with a secure and convenient means of reporting.  ", "businessDrivers4": "The public benefits the Client Reporting and Engagement System (CRES)/ReportIn system is expected to have:
•	Improve and modernize interactions the CBSA has with individuals subject to reporting requirements.
•	Improve the CBSA monitoring of individuals on reporting conditions through improved identity verification and location services which may decrease the number of warrants issued and benefit the public by meeting our public safety mandate.
•	The CBSA will reuse information technology micro-service sub-systems across government services and departments rather than procuring new systems and technology which will provide cost savings benefits to the public.
•	Provide a remote reporting option for individuals on reporting conditions. This will decrease the number of in-person reporting individuals, which will in turn reduce the time CBSA staff meet with individuals providing additional cost savings to the public.", "businessDrivers5": "item3-0", "businessDrivers6": "The improvements, benefits and advantages the CBSA expect from using the CRES/ReportIn are:
•	Enhancing compliance by individuals with an easy to use voluntary app using facial biometrics comparison for identification purposes.
•	Reducing detention costs by providing access to a remote reporting ATD.
•	Enhancing CBSA relations with individuals by facilitating a secure process for individuals to enroll and report without coming in person to a CBSA office.
•	Allowing CBSA to more effectively monitor individuals who report remotely through submission of the individual’s location at the same time as the submission of the report photos. 
•	Having the ability to receive regular updates from the individual through the app on their residential address, employment, among other things, will allow the CBSA to have relevant information on the individual that can be used to contact and monitor them for any early indicators of non-compliance.
•	Allowing for, when appropriate, an accessible alternative to in-person reporting requirements, and having an enhanced verification and monitoring system.
•	Building and maintaining relationships with CBSA and individuals will ensure individuals are fully aware of their obligations and have a process for getting timely answers to any questions about the status of their case.
•	Providing CBSA the ability to track trends and patterns of individuals (e.g. individuals that may attempt to hide or falsify their identity by submitting photos obscuring their face or providing photos of another individual, attempt to hide or falsify their location, etc.).
•	Reducing non-compliance of individuals by providing a more reliable remote reporting option that can be accessed by more individuals that will allow anyone with a smartphone or mobile device with access to Wi-Fi or cellular data to use the app.", "businessDrivers7": "The CBSA will ensure the system is confined to address individual needs by:  
•	The CRES/ReportIn will be rolled out as a tool that enables CBSA officers to more effectively manage individuals within the community as a means to support the ATD mandate. It will provide a safe and secure remote reporting option to submit 5 facial view photos and their point-in-time location information while ensuring that individuals are not constantly tracked.   
•	Individuals required to report to the CBSA will be assessed for eligibility for ReportIn by a CBSA officer. The assessment will include, but is not limited to, the individual having a smartphone or mobile device and be willing to comply with the reporting conditions. Positive eligibility will provide enhanced accessibility to remote reporting and will provide individual financial cost savings (e.g. not having to in person report, take time off work, find childcare and/or reduce transportation costs).
•	CBSA has developed step-by-step instructions, including screenshots, for the voluntary ReportIn app and how to: download the app; create an account in the app; report and submit photos in the app; and get assistance with the app.  
•	The voluntary ReportIn app is available in 16 languages, including English and French, in order to allow individuals to have greater accessibility using the app and increase compliance with reporting conditions with the app.  
•	CBSA’s ReportIn Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) and Privacy Policy provides individuals with the appropriate safeguards for their facial biometrics information which will not be used for any other purpose (including by the supplier) than stated.
•	CBSA’s contract with Amazon Web Service (AWS) includes a nondisclosure agreement providing additional safeguards for individuals’ information using remote reporting through ReportIn. AWS will not access, use or disclose personal information except as needed to provide services under contract and/or to provide notifications of personal data breaches.
•	Using ReportIn, an app that works with Wi-Fi, will save individuals money by not requiring a data plan for connectivity and will allow a greater number of individuals to have access to an alternative to detention and an alternative to reporting in person to a CBSA office; although a data plan may also be used.
•	CBSA staff will receive training on the use of the one-way messaging feature used to contact individuals. The training will provide CBSA staff instructions that all contact with individuals is to be in compliance with the Public Service Values and Ethics and CBSA Code of Conduct.  ", "businessDrivers8": "•	CRES/ReportIn is accessible to a wide range of individuals and individuals are more likely to comply when there is an automated system available for remote reporting compared to in-person reporting where CBSA are more likely to see an increase in individual non-compliance due to several factors e.g. transportation costs, loss of employment time, child care issues and costs, etc.
•	The voluntary app will collect facial biometrics and an accurate location, which may be perceived as an impact on the privacy of individuals on reporting condition. This collection of information was weighed against the objectives of the ATD program and the requirements of the IRPA and IRPR.  ", "businessDrivers9": "item1-0", "businessDrivers10": "Yes non-automated processes have been considered, specifically individual in-person reporting. In-person reporting has been identified by individuals to be a hardship for them based on employment needs, childcare needs, cost of transportation, time for transportation (e.g. individuals residing in northern Saskatchewan or Manitoba may have to travel 6 to 8 hours by vehicle to attend a CBSA office in person).  The remote reporting voluntary app will allow for more individuals to access an ATD in a timely fashion.

In-person reporting is still an option for those individuals that are determined by the CBSA to not be eligible for remote reporting, for those individuals that do not or cannot report remotely and for those individuals that do not choose or do not agree to use remote reporting as an option.

Individuals are more likely to comply when there is an automated system available for remote reporting compared to in-person reporting. This is based on direct complaints from individuals, where CBSA are more likely to see an increase in individual non-compliance due to several factors e.g. transportation costs, loss of employment time, child care issues and costs, etc.", "businessDrivers11": ["item6-0", "item2-2", "item3-0", "item1-3", "item4-0"], "businessDrivers12": "The other consequence of not deploying the CRES/ReportIn solution may lead to more individuals having to report in person, being detained or remaining in detention if an appropriate ATD system is not implemented.", "riskProfile1": "item1-3", "riskProfile2": "item1-3", "riskProfile3": "item2-0", "riskProfile4": "item2-0", "riskProfile5": "item2-0", "projectAuthority1": "item2-0", "aboutSystem1": ["item1", "item5"], "aboutAlgorithm1": "item1-3", "aboutAlgorithm2": "item1-3", "decisionSector1": ["item6-1"], "decisionSector2": "The CRES/ReportIn voluntary app is an ATD system that may allow individuals, subject to reporting conditions under the IRPA or IRPR, to report remotely rather than to be detained in a facility or as an option to be released from detention. loi", "projectDetailsTitle": "Client Reporting and Engagement System (CRES/ReportIn) / Système de Rapports et d’engagement des Clients (SREC)/JeMePrésente", "businessDrivers1": ["item1", "item4", "item5"], "decisionSector3": "The decision that will be automated is the facial comparison portion of the process.**

ReportIn uses Amazon Rekognition, a facial recognition software, to perform a comparison of an individual’s report photo against an individual’s reference photo (known individual) stored in CRES. This comparison generates a similarity score, between the enrollment/reference photo and an individual’s report photo, indicating a percentage similarity score of likelihood that the individual who is reporting is the individual on reporting conditions as indicated by their enrollment/reference photo. The default similarity score for a photo match is 99%.  If a score of under 99% is returned from the software, the result is failed biometrics. If a score of over 99% is returned from the software, the result is passed biometrics. There are several reasons that a result may return at less than 99% and failed biometrics, such as but not limited to, the angle of the face is less than or more than expected, the photo is blurry, the photo is that of another person, etc.

The software analyzes the facial attributes of the face, and then returns a percent similarity score for the face and the facial attributes that are detected in the image e.g. beard, eyeglasses, eyes open, landmarks of the face (e.g. position of nose, mouth).

It is important to note that the software does not compare images against a database of publicly collected images.

All submissions will undergo 100% human oversight by a CBSA employee. This approach ensures that human judgment and discretion are applied to the results, promoting accountability and accuracy in the system's operation.

After the software similarity score is returned and displayed in CRES, each case and/or report is assigned to an ATD MCO to review. The ATD MCO makes the decision on whether or not the report photo is a ‘match’ or ‘no match’. The ‘match’ or ‘no match’ decision is based on the ATD MCO’s determination of the photo match and, when a specified location to report from has been imposed, the individual’s location when they reported. There are three levels of review. All cases and reports will be assigned to an ATD MCO for review. If an individual fails a facial comparison match or location match and the ATD MCO is not able to make a final decision that there is a match, the ATD MCO will contact the individual within a day or two for a follow up investigation. If the ATD MCO still has questions they will refer it to the ATD MCS, if the ATD MCS has additional questions they will refer it to the Regional Inland Enforcement office for investigation by an Inland Enforcement Officer (IEO). If the follow up investigation results in the IEO deciding that the individual has not complied with their conditions, the individual could be detained as per the IRPA and the IRPR.", "impact31": "The decision that will be automated is the facial comparison portion of the process.**

The process begins with CBSA staff determining eligibility of an individual for remote reporting. When an individual is eligible, CBSA staff provide an explanation of ReportIn to the client and obtains the client consent for remote reporting. Once consent is received, CBSA staff obtain the current client information of telephone number and email address.  

During the enrollment process, the CBSA staff verifies the individual during an in-person or virtual meeting. The individual is advised to download the voluntary ReportIn app onto their smartphone or mobile device.  Once the app is downloaded, the individual is instructed to create their account using their first name, last name, email address and telephone number. For authentication purposes, the individual is asked to input their UCI as well as a Multifactor Authentication (MFA) code sent to their email address. After successfully creating their account in ReportIn, the individual is instructed to review their contact information displayed in the app which was created in CRES and transmitted to ReportIn.  If the information is correct, the client clicks yes and continues to the next step.  

The individual is instructed to take 5 facial photo views in a randomized order (looking up, looking down, looking left, looking right and looking straight ahead). The facial photos taken at this step are the individual’s enrollment photos. CBSA staff choose this facial photo, looking straight ahead, as the reference facial photo and avatar photo for the individual in CRES. This reference photo can be changed in time due to facial changes such as, but not limited to, plastic surgery, accident, etc.

Once the 5 photo views are taken for enrollment, the individual is instructed to complete their first report. The following steps are completed:  log into ReportIn with their email address and password, verify their personal information and submit their 5 facial photo views to report. Once the 5 facial photo views are submitted in ReportIn, the 5 facial photos are sent to Amazon Rekognition for facial comparison.  

For background, the software offers pre-trained and customizable computer vision (CV) capabilities to extract information and insights from the individual submitted images.

The software analyzes the facial attributes of the face, and then returns a percent similarity score for the face and the facial attributes that are detected in the image e.g. beard, eyeglasses, eyes open, landmarks of the face (e.g. position of nose, mouth).

**ReportIn uses Amazon Rekognition, a facial recognition software, to perform a comparison of an individual’s reported photo against an individual’s referenced photo (known individual) stored in CRES. This comparison generates a similarity score, between the enrollment/reference photo and an individual’s report photo, indicating a percentage similarity score of likelihood that the individual who is reporting is the individual on reporting conditions as indicated by their enrollment/reference photo. The default similarity score for a photo match is 99%. If a score of under 99% is returned from the software, the result is failed biometrics. If a score of over 99% is returned from the software, the result is passed biometrics. There are several reasons that a result may return at less than 99% and failed biometrics, such as but not limited to, the angle of the face is less than or more than expected, the photo is blurry, the photo is that of another person, etc.

It is important to note that the software does not compare images against a database of publicly collected images.

All submissions will undergo 100% human oversight. This approach ensures that human judgment and discretion are applied to the results, promoting accountability and accuracy in the system's operation. 

After the software similarity score is returned and displayed in CRES, each case and/or report is assigned to an ATD Monitoring Centre Officer (MCO) to review. The ATD MCO makes the decision on whether or not the report photo is a ‘match’ or ‘no match’. The ‘match’ or ‘no match’ decision is based on the ATD MCO’s determination of the photo match and, when a specified location to report from has been imposed, the individual’s location when they reported.  

There are three levels of review. All cases and reports will be assigned to an ATD MCO for review. If an individual fails a facial comparison match or location match and the ATD MCO is not able to make a final decision that there is a match, the ATD MCO will contact the individual within a day or two a follow up investigation. If the ATD MCO still has questions they will refer it to the ATD MCS, if the ATD MCS has additional questions they will refer it to the Regional Inland Enforcement office for investigation by an IEO. If the follow up investigation results in the IEO deciding that the individual has not complied with their conditions, the individual could be detained as per the IRPA and the IRPR.", "impact20": "After the individual downloads the voluntary ReportIn app and creates their account in ReportIn, at the enrollment stage, with the CBSA staff present, 5 facial photos views of an individual’s face are taken through the ReportIn app of the individual at various angles in randomized order and then uploaded to CRES.
Looking straight ahead
Looking up
Looking down
Left side of the face
Right side of the face

As per set reporting conditions, at each report the individual will provide 5 new photos. These photos will be requested in a random order each time in the ReportIn app.
Looking straight ahead
Looking up
Looking down
Left side of the face
Right side of the face

Once the 5 photo views are taken for enrollment, the individual is instructed to complete their first report. The following steps are completed:  log into ReportIn with their email address and password, verify their personal information and submit their 5 photo views to report. Once the 5 photo views are submitted in ReportIn, they are sent to Amazon Rekognition for facial comparison.  

ReportIn uses Amazon Rekognition, a facial recognition software, to perform a comparison of an individual’s report photo against an individual’s reference photo (known individual) stored in CRES. This comparison generates a similarity score, between the enrollment/reference photo and an individual’s report photo, indicating a percentage similarity score of likelihood that the individual who is reporting is the individual on reporting conditions as indicated by their enrollment/reference photo. The default similarity score for a photo match is 99%. If a score of under 99% is returned from the software, the result is failed biometrics. If a score of over 99% is returned from the software, the result is passed biometrics. There are several reasons that a result may return at less than 99% and failed biometrics, such as but not limited to, the angle of the face is less than or more than expected, the photo is blurry, the photo is that of another person, etc.

It is important to note that the software does not compare images against a database of publicly collected images.

After the software similarity score is returned and displayed in CRES, each case and or report is assigned to an ATD MCO to review. The ATD MCO makes the decision on whether or not the report photo is a ‘match’ or ‘no match’. The ‘match’ or ‘no match’ decision is based on the ATD MCO’s determination of the photo match and, when a specified location to report from has been imposed, the individual’s location when they reported.

All CBSA staff using CRES are required to take the CBSA Facial Comparison Course prior to access being granted access to CRES as part of their job duties.

To further mitigate the risk of false results, all submissions will have 100% human oversight by a CBSA employee. CBSA staff will review the individual’s reference photo and the current report photo against the software results to assess and address potential accuracy, bias and other performance issues associated with the software. The backend system CRES will store the images including the enrollment photo, reference photo and report photos.

After the software similarity score is returned and displayed in CRES, each case and/or report is assigned to an ATD MCO to review. The ATD MCO makes the decision on whether or not the report photo is a ‘match’ or ‘no match’. The ‘match’ or ‘no match’ decision is based on the ATD MCO’s determination of the photo match and, when a specified location to report from has been imposed, the individual’s location when they reported.  

There are three levels of review. All cases and reports will be assigned to an ATD MCO for review.  If an individual fails a facial comparison match or location match and the ATD MCO is not able to make a final decision that there is a match, the ATD MCO will contact the individual within a day or two a follow up investigation. If the ATD MCO still has questions they will refer it to the ATD MCS, if the ATD MCS has additional questions they will refer it to the Regional Inland Enforcement office for investigation by an IEO. If the follow up investigation results in the IEO deciding that the individual has not complied with their conditions, the individual could be detained as per the IRPA and the IRPR.

The similarity default score for a photo match is 99%. If a score of under 99% is returned from the software, the result is failed biometrics. If a score of over 99% is returned from the software, the result is passed biometrics. There are several reasons that a result may return at less than 99% and failed biometrics, such as but not limited to, the angle of the face is less than or more than expected, the photo is blurry, the photo is that of another person, etc.  After the software similarity score is returned and displayed in CRES, each case and or report is assigned to an ATD MCO to review.  

The ATD MCO makes the decision on whether or not the report photo is a ‘match’ or ‘no match’. The ‘match’ or ‘no match’ decision is based on the ATD MCO’s determination of the photo match.  ", "impact8": "The impacts are reversible at each of the three levels of review. If an individual fails the facial photo comparison for scoring under 99%, their file is triaged as a priority in the assignment of cases to the ATD MCO for review. The ATD MCO of the ATD MC reviews the file, if they suspect the facial comparison is not a match, the file is then reviewed by the ATD MCS. If they are also in agreement, the file is transferred to the Regional Inland Enforcement office for investigation. During the investigation process, the individual will be provided an opportunity to provide information/evidence in person.  

Example: individual had facial surgery, are in transition, etc. The Regional IEO will review all the information/evidence, depending on the conclusion of the investigation, the individual may be transferred to other ATD options or continue with remote reporting. 

All submissions will undergo 100% human oversight. The ATD MCO results will be analyzed to assess potential accuracy versus software results. This approach ensures that human judgment and discretion are applied to the results, promoting accountability and accuracy in the system's operation. The ATD MCO will be making the final decision, not the system. 

The ATD MCO makes the final decision on whether or not the report photo is a ‘match’ or ‘no match’.  If one of the photo views fails the biometric match by the software but the remainder of the photo views pass the biometric match, the ATD MCO reviewing the file has the ability to make the overall decision as a ‘match’ or ‘no match’ decision and is based on the ATD MCO’s determination.  ", "impact10": "Amazon has conducted a third party (Credo AI) review of the face recognition software to validate the performance and extract any inclinations of bias based on a variety of demographic groups, the results of the evaluation were 99.9% facial match rate across 6 different demographic groups. It is also important to note that this software is facial comparison based off of a photo provided by CBSA. All individuals enrolled in ReportIn are voluntarily enrolled and provide consent to the use of their likeness to verify their identity. The app uses technology akin to the facial unlocking mechanism found in smartphones.
 
To further mitigate the risk of false results, all submissions will have 100% human oversight, and a comparison of the ATD MCO results versus software results will be conducted to assess and address potential accuracy, bias and other performance issues associated with the software. This approach ensures that human judgment and discretion are applied to the results, promoting accountability and accuracy in the system's operation.  Additionally, CBSA Communications has been consulted to develop comprehensive and informative materials, which will be posted publicly to address potential concerns surrounding the use of the technology and other performance issues.

The ATD MCO makes the final decision on whether or not the report photo is a ‘match’ or ‘no match’.  If one of the photo views fails the biometric match by the software but the remainder of the photo views pass the biometric match, the ATD MCO reviewing the file has the ability to make the overall decision as a ‘match’ or ‘no match’ decision is based on the ATD MCO’s determination. Therefore, there is a low impact on the health of the individual.", "impact14": "If an individual fails the facial photo comparison, their file is assigned to an ATD MCO for review.  Once the ATD MCO of the ATD MC reviews the file, and they suspect the facial comparison is not a match, the file is then reviewed by the ATD MCS. If they are also in agreement, the file is transferred to the Regional Inland Enforcement office for investigation. During the investigation process, the individual will be provided an opportunity to provide information/evidence in person in regards to the investigation. Example: individual had facial surgery, etc. Depending on the conclusion of the investigation, the individual may be transferred to other alternative to detention options.  

To further mitigate the risk of false results, all submissions will have 100% human oversight, and a comparison of the ATD MCO’s results versus software results will be conducted to assess and address potential accuracy, bias and other performance issues associated with the software. Additionally, CBSA Communications has been consulted to develop comprehensive and informative materials that address potential concerns surrounding the use of the technology and other performance issues. This approach ensures that human judgment and discretion are applied to the results, promoting accountability and accuracy in the system's operation.

The individual is unaware of the decision unless or until they are contacted by the Regional Inland Enforcement office during their investigation therefore the likelihood of the individual’s economic interests being at risk is low. The investigation may be dealt with at a lower level by the ATD MCO or ATD MCS or by desk investigation which would have a lower financial impact on the individual. The ATD MCO will try to facilitate any interviews around the individuals schedule and pose minimal financial impact to the individual thus improving supports to individuals with accessibility needs where they do not need to attend a CBSA office by reducing: child care cost, reduced transportation cost and/or travel time etc.    
 
The ATD MCO makes the final decision on whether or not the report photo is a ‘match’ or ‘no match’.  If one of the photo views fails the biometric match by the software but the remainder of the photo views pass the biometric match, the ATD MCO reviewing the file has the ability to make the overall decision as a ‘match’ or ‘no match’ decision is based on the ATD MCO’s determination. CBSA has a contract with AWS that outlines all privacy parameters including storage.", "projectDetailsRespondent": "Bradley Belanger", "projectDetailsJob": "Director General / Directeur général", "privacyImplementation5": "Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)
Remote Reporting Program

CRES/ReportIn PIA is has been completed.
The scope of this PIA is to provide an overview of the CRES/ReportIn solution that has been established for the ATD Program, and assess the privacy risks associated with the introduction of the CRES/ReportIn solution.

Specifically, the scope of this PIA includes the following:
•	An overview of the upcoming use of the CRES/ReportIn solution
•	Facial comparison is used as a biometric identifier 

This description includes the following:
•	The systems used to support the CRES/ReportIn solution;
•	CRES/ReportIn events and subsequent activities related to evaluation and assignment of an investigation event in the National Case Management System;
•	The storage, retention and accessibility of CRES/ReportIn data.

In support of efforts to expand and improve ATDs, the CBSA has developed a voluntary smartphone app called ReportIn that allows individuals subject to immigration enforcement conditions to report to the CBSA remotely. Only those individuals expressly required to report to the CBSA as a condition of their release and choose ReportIn as the means of reporting will have access to create an account.

The CBSA will be launching CRES/ReportIn in Fall 2024 on a phased-in basis to provide enough time for the orderly transition of individuals. The app will allow for a reduction in in-person reporting or detention in situations where no current appropriate ATD exists. With the app, the CBSA also has an opportunity to build a more nationally consistent ATD that will be secure and more widely available.

The CRES/ReportIn solution is made up by the following major components: Smartphone Application: Prior to initializing the sign up process, the individual must download the CBSA ReportIn mobile app from either the Apple App store or the Google Play store, based upon the individuals’ device they are using. In order to protect the individuals’ Personal Identifiable Information (PII), a Secure Remote Password (SRP) is used between the individuals’ device and the CRES AWS environment.  Amazon Cognito is used as an authentication provider that supports OAuth 2.0, Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML) and SRP. The mobile app utilizes AWS Amplify to interface with Amazon Cognito using SRP. Through the Cognito SRP protocol, the mobile app is utilized to implement user sign-up, user sign-in, multi factor authentication and forgot password functionality, minimizing snooping or “Man in the Middle” attack risks.

CRES Web App & Database: The main processing and storage hub for the ReportIn solution is the CRES database, used to store ReportIn individual authentication data, and the web application. The web application is used by the CBSA staff to review and manage the active cases of individuals, as well as providing ongoing as well as historical reporting capabilities. The CRES console is the main interface used by CBSA staff and provides the staff the ability to manage multiple individuals and cases in a centralized location.

Facial recognition software: To validate the individual’s biometric facial data, the app requires the individual to provide multiple biometric facial photos that are used to identify the individual for the duration of their ATD enrollment. The Amazon Rekognition software is used to perform this authentication/validation requirement. The biometric facial photos are hosted in the AWS Government in Canada (GC) Private cloud located within Canadian datacenters to meet data residence requirements.", "projectDetailsID": "PRJ-00005299", "projectDetailsProgram": "Border Enforcement / Detentions / Exécution de la loi à la frontière / Détentions", "aboutDataSource8": "item1-1"}
